text: the Role of Play in Civilization We want to discuss games of chance, but we must keep in mind that any analysis of such games must be placed within the wider context of ‘play’ and the role of the playful in human evolution and contemporary culture. In terms of overall categorization, one can here refer to the work of Roger Caillois who usefully divided games into four main categories: agon, alea, mimicry and illinx (Caillois 2001: 12). Caillois’s classification follows from the degree of presence in each of them of three main elements: competition, chance and simulation. Agon covers sports and games in which the player actively attempts to direct the outcome of the contest or game (chess being an obvious example). Alea is 3 There is perhaps an extent to which this actually presents an empirical challenge to Girard’s theory of the scapegoat, for in the biblical text there is little trace of the social mechanisms of identifying the victim; Girard’s ‘persecuting crowd’ seems replaced by a lottery. Liminality and the Modern 146 primarily characterized by the element of chance. The player is (relatively at least) passive and surrenders to destiny. The most evident examples of alea are lotteries, roulette and dice games. Mimicry is characterized by the involvement of fantasy and suspension of reality on the part of the participant. Children’s games are the primary examples here. Lastly, ilinx involves an element of vertigo, gymnastic activities, skiing and mountain climbing being examples. Significantly, Caillois identifies ilinx by a ‘rapid whirling or falling movement’ (ibid.). In the following chapter we will discuss ilinx with reference to exactly such a ‘whirling-falling’, exemplified by bungee jumping practices. Here, however, it is primarily with alea, games of chance, that we are concerned. Categories aside, it must always be kept in mind that games of chance are a subcategory of play, and cannot be easily disentangled from the general evolution of the playful in civilization. This opens up a theme so broad that it can hardly be dealt with within one book, let alone a single chapter. Luckily we have good guides at our service, not least Johan Huizinga (1872–1945), the eminent Dutch historian, whose most famous work was of course Homo Ludens, and which elegantly laid out the underlying features of play, on the basis of which Caillois refined and formulated the above distinctions. Johan Huizinga and Norbert Elias on Play Civilization gradually brings about a certain division between two modes of mental life which we distinguish as play and seriousness respectively, but which originally formed a continuous mental medium wherein that civilization arose. (Johan Huizinga, Homo Ludens) As a thinker, Huizinga was always well-prepared to engage with the concept of play. Huizinga was trained as a linguist, and wrote his doctoral thesis on the role of the clown figure (or jester) in Sanskrit drama in 1897, before he turned to the study of Renaissance and early modern history. In all of his works, art, ritual and spectacle were identified as crucial; he perceived how artistic and ‘aestethic forms’ went to the heart of social life and attitudes, much more so than any socio- economic indicator. That is why he is seen as a founding father of cultural history. Although he was an close contemporary of Arnold van Gennep (who was born just four months later than Huizinga) he never encountered his work. Huizinga is famous for three works: In the The Waning of the Middle Ages he famously reinterpreted the later Middle Ages as a period of pessimism and decadence rather than rebirth. In 1924 he published a book on Erasmus (a non-player if ever there was one), whereas Homo Ludens appeared in 1938. The timing of the publication must be noted. Although Huizinga in Homo Ludens is not very explicit concerning the drama of the contemporary scene, there is no doubt that the motivating energy behind the book was a recognition that something had gone terribly wrong with the element of play in culture, and that this ‘something’ was connected to the rise of mass movements and totalitarian regimes, and to a profound cultural crisis – one from which we certainly cannot declare Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 147 immunity today. In other writings he was more explicit about these connections. In 1938 Huizinga also became vice-president of the International Committee of Intellectual Cooperation with the League of Nations. Alarmed by the rise of fascism and the spread of a deep-bound cultural crisis, Huizinga had written In de schaduwen van morgen (1935) which his son Jacob Herman Huizinga translated into English and published in 1936 under the title In the Shadow of Tomorrow. He was then working on Homo Ludens. The Dutch subtitle was A diagnosis of the spiritual suffering [leiden] of our times. The first chapter, Apprehensions of Doom, opens with these lines: We live in a demented [bezeten] world. And we know it. It would not come as a surprise to anyone if tomorrow the madness gave way to a frenzy which would leave our poor Europe in a state of distracted stupor, with engines still turning and flags streaming in the breeze, but with the spirit gone. [...] Everywhere there are doubts as to the solidity of our social structure, vague fears of the imminent future, a feeling that our civilization is on the way to ruin. (Huizinga 1936: 15) Evidently, Huizinga wrote Homo Ludens with the consciousness that old Europe was approaching its end, in a highly liminal moment. He was translating it into English and adding material right after the Nazis invaded Poland. He clearly intuited similarities with the psycho-social abendnacht moods during the ‘waning’ of the Middle Ages and the existential uncertainty and spread of violence that marked the present age. 4 Huizinga sensed a proliferating decay, and it was spreading even from within the two highly celebrated institutional achievements of modernity, namely universal education and the public sphere: Our time [is] faced by the discouraging fact that two
